questions.text: I hold the view that processing suffering analytically is a practical necessity in any context where the stakes are high and the margin for error is small.
When facing a decision, I often go with my first instinct rather than deliberating at length.
I believe that my steady internal state is one of the things that allows me to be present and attentive.
I have a strong instinct to organize people toward a goal.
I find it satisfying to bring order to something that was previously disorganized.
I protect focused work time by blocking it on my calendar so no one can interrupt it.
I hold the view that maintaining my own well-being is a prerequisite for everything else.
I find it rewarding when a discussion closes with genuine shared understanding.
I rarely detect when a friendly rivalry has an edge of real competition underneath.
I think of myself as someone who facilitates outcomes.
I experience the world through a lens of emotional vividness that rarely dims.
I routinely take credit for my contributions because acknowledging my own work matters to me.
I notice when someone repeats a phrase another person used earlier to build rapport or to mock them.
I keep spare supplies on hand so I never run out of essentials.
I usually do not notice when someone adjusts their behavior after getting a social signal from someone else.
I am the one who defines success criteria for the group.
I believe that my emotional steadiness is something that has always been true about me, even as a child.
I prefer to enter conversations without a script so I can respond authentically.
I often do not realize that a conversation had multiple layers until someone explains it to me afterward.
I hold the view that the moral weight of someone's suffering, once I have genuinely registered it, creates a responsibility I cannot delegate.
I feel a need to put my stamp on everything I am involved in.
I think that the most reliable path to helping others in distress is through careful, dispassionate analysis of the situation.
I find that I'm easily stirred by stories, films, or events that others take in stride.
I detect when the way someone closes a door, gently or firmly, carries emotional information.
I believe that the weight I assign to others' suffering is the single most important variable in determining the quality of my moral life.
I am drawn to roles where I support and enable others' success.
I feel that the highest form of contribution is quiet, unrecognized service.
I have noticed that I recover from exposure to others' suffering faster than most people around me.
I find that my threshold for emotional activation is simply lower than most people I know.
I tend to navigate most situations without noticeable shifts in my internal state.
I rarely review past expenses because I trust my general sense of where I stand financially.
I feel that my own happiness is something I should actively build and protect.
I hold the view that my time is best invested in service to the people around me.
I often notice that a person's feet or hands tell a different story than their words.
I feel that the most valuable thing I own is my willingness to share it.
I believe that the felt weight of suffering is the moral force that prevents me from treating people as abstractions.
